out_of_scope_components: Tests, scripts and other code or documentation that cannot be exploited when the client is running
Anything that cannot be exploited on a live network
scope_notes: Only Fusaka-specific code is in scope. Any vulnerabilities not specifically related to the upgrade should be submitted via the Ethereum Foundation's Bug Bounty Program.
Vulnerabilities requiring the user to have publicly exposed an API, such as JSON-RPC or the Beacon API, are explicitly out-of-scope.
High-effort (sustained, CPU or bandwidth intensive, and/or requires more than 1 packet or on-chain transaction) single-peer DoS attacks are out-of-scope.
All submissions (except informational) must include an executable coded proof of concept at the time of submission.
Severity is measured by the finding's impact on the entire Ethereum network.
EIP-7934 (RLP Execution Block Size Limit) and EIP-7825 (Transaction Gas Limit Cap) are relevant in-scope EIPs for this trust model.

actors.name: Transaction Sender
actors.description: External account or smart contract that creates and submits transactions to the network. The sender crafts transaction payloads including gas parameters, destination, value, data, blob data, and authorization lists. Fully untrusted -- any externally-owned account can submit arbitrary transaction data.

actors.name: Block Proposer / Validator
actors.description: Validator selected by the consensus layer to propose a block. The proposer assembles transactions, constructs the block header (including gas used, state root, receipt root, blob gas used, requests hash), and submits the block for validation. Semi-trusted because proposers are economically incentivized via staking but can include arbitrary valid transactions and may attempt to construct malicious blocks.

actors.name: Consensus Layer (CL)
actors.description: The Beacon Chain consensus client that communicates with the Execution Layer via Engine API. Provides parent_beacon_block_root, withdrawals list, and triggers block validation. Semi-trusted as it is co-located and JWT-authenticated, but data from CL (e.g., withdrawals, beacon roots) drives state changes.

actors.name: System Address (SYSTEM_ADDRESS)
actors.description: Special privileged address (0xff...fe) used for system transactions. System transactions interact with pre-deployed system contracts (beacon roots, history storage, withdrawal requests, consolidation requests). Trusted by the protocol -- system transactions bypass normal fee and nonce checks.

actors.name: System Contracts (Pre-deployed)
actors.description: Pre-deployed contracts at well-known addresses: BEACON_ROOTS_ADDRESS, HISTORY_STORAGE_ADDRESS, WITHDRAWAL_REQUEST_ADDRESS, CONSOLIDATION_REQUEST_ADDRESS. These contracts are deployed at genesis/fork and execute during block processing. Trusted as their code is defined in the protocol specification.

actors.name: P2P Network Peer
actors.description: Any node on the P2P network that propagates blocks and transactions. Peers can send arbitrary data including malformed blocks, invalid transactions, and oversized payloads. Fully untrusted -- no authentication is required to join the P2P network.

actors.name: EVM Execution Engine
actors.description: The Ethereum Virtual Machine that executes transaction message calls, processes opcodes, and produces execution outputs (gas used, logs, state changes, refund counters). Trusted component internal to the node, but bugs in its implementation can have critical consequences.

actors.name: State Database
actors.description: Persistent storage for account balances, nonces, code, and storage slots. Accessed via get_account, modify_state, set_account_balance, trie_set operations. Trusted as an internal data store, but integrity depends on correct state transition logic.

trust_boundaries.description: Transaction submission to the execution layer. Transaction payloads include type, gas parameters (gas_limit, max_fee_per_gas, max_priority_fee_per_gas, max_fee_per_blob_gas), destination address, value, calldata, blob versioned hashes, and authorization lists. The validate_transaction and check_transaction functions (SG-004, SG-006) enforce all validation rules before the transaction reaches the EVM.
trust_boundaries.data_flow: Transaction type, gas parameters, nonce, destination (to), value, calldata, blob_versioned_hashes, authorization_list, signature (v, r, s)

trust_boundaries.description: Block propagation via P2P network. Peers send full blocks including headers and transaction lists. The state_transition function (SG-003) validates the entire block including RLP size (MAX_RLP_BLOCK_SIZE via EIP-7934), header fields, and all computed roots/hashes. A malicious peer can craft blocks with invalid headers, oversized RLP, or inconsistent root hashes.
trust_boundaries.data_flow: RLP-encoded blocks, block headers, transaction lists, withdrawal lists

trust_boundaries.description: Engine API delivering block parameters from CL to EL. The CL provides parent_beacon_block_root (used in beacon roots system transaction), withdrawals list (processed by process_withdrawals SG-014), and block environment parameters. Data crosses the JWT-authenticated Engine API boundary.
trust_boundaries.data_flow: parent_beacon_block_root, withdrawals list (validator_index, address, amount), block parameters (timestamp, number, coinbase, gas_limit, base_fee_per_gas, excess_blob_gas)

trust_boundaries.description: System transactions from SYSTEM_ADDRESS to pre-deployed system contracts. process_unchecked_system_transaction (SG-010) and process_checked_system_transaction (SG-009) invoke system contracts. Unchecked variants do not verify contract code existence or execution success, while checked variants enforce both. The apply_body function (SG-011) executes beacon roots and history storage as unchecked, while withdrawal and consolidation requests are checked.
trust_boundaries.data_flow: System transaction calldata (parent_beacon_block_root to BEACON_ROOTS_ADDRESS, parent_hash to HISTORY_STORAGE_ADDRESS), return data from WITHDRAWAL_REQUEST_ADDRESS and CONSOLIDATION_REQUEST_ADDRESS

trust_boundaries.description: Block header fields used in state transition validation. The validate_header function (SG-005) checks block header fields against computed values. A malicious proposer can set header fields (gas_used, state_root, transactions_root, receipt_root, bloom, withdrawals_root, blob_gas_used, requests_hash) that must match the locally computed values exactly. The state_transition function (SG-003) verifies all 8 roots/hashes.
trust_boundaries.data_flow: Block header fields: number, timestamp, parent_hash, gas_limit, gas_used, base_fee_per_gas, excess_blob_gas, extra_data, difficulty, nonce, ommers_hash, state_root, transactions_root, receipt_root, bloom, withdrawals_root, blob_gas_used, requests_hash

trust_boundaries.description: Transaction-driven state modifications. The process_transaction function (SG-013) modifies state: increments sender nonce, deducts gas fees and blob gas fees, executes the transaction, applies gas refunds (capped at gas_used//5 per EIP-3529), enforces calldata floor gas cost (EIP-7623), pays the coinbase priority fees, and destroys accounts marked for deletion.
trust_boundaries.data_flow: Balance changes (sender deduction, coinbase payment, refunds), nonce increments, storage modifications, account creation/deletion, log entries

trust_boundaries.description: Withdrawal processing from CL-provided withdrawal list. The process_withdrawals function (SG-014) iterates over withdrawals and increases account balances. Withdrawal amounts are scaled by 10^9 (Gwei to Wei). Balances are only ever increased, never decreased.
trust_boundaries.data_flow: Withdrawal entries: validator_index, address, amount (in Gwei, scaled to Wei)

trust_boundaries.description: Blob transaction data submitted by users. The blob_transaction_validation (SG-005) and check_transaction blob checks validate: blob_versioned_hashes non-empty, count <= MAX_BLOB_COUNT, total_blob_gas within limit, max_fee_per_blob_gas >= blob_base_fee, and all hash versions == VERSIONED_HASH_VERSION_KZG (0x01). This is a critical boundary for data availability.

SetCode (EIP-7702) authorization list boundary. Transactions of type SetCode include authorization lists that delegate code execution. check_transaction (SG-006) validates that authorization lists are non-empty for SetCode transactions and that senders with code must have valid delegation designations.
trust_boundaries.data_flow: Authorization list entries (chain_id, address, nonce, y_parity, r, s), delegation designations

trust_boundaries.description: RLP-encoded block size validation boundary (EIP-7934). The state_transition function (SG-003) enforces MAX_RLP_BLOCK_SIZE check before any other validation. A peer sending an oversized block triggers immediate rejection. This is a Fusaka-specific defense against resource exhaustion.

General purpose request processing. The process_general_purpose_requests function (SG-012) parses deposit requests from block output logs and calls checked system transactions for withdrawal and consolidation requests. The request ordering (deposits -> withdrawals -> consolidations) is an invariant. Withdrawal and consolidation system transactions must succeed or the block is invalid.
trust_boundaries.data_flow: Deposit request logs, withdrawal request return data, consolidation request return data, request type prefixes

trust_boundaries.description: Gas limit adjustment validation boundary. The check_gas_limit function (SG-015) and calculate_base_fee_per_gas (SG-004 of fork.py) enforce that gas limit changes between blocks are bounded by parent_gas_limit // GAS_LIMIT_ADJUSTMENT_FACTOR (1024), gas limit >= GAS_LIMIT_MINIMUM (5000), and base fee is adjusted according to the EIP-1559 algorithm. A proposer controls the gas_limit field within these bounds.
trust_boundaries.data_flow: block gas_limit, parent gas_limit, parent gas_used, base_fee_per_gas

assumptions.text: Transaction signatures (ECDSA secp256k1) are unforgeable. The recover_sender function correctly derives the sender address from the transaction signature. If signature recovery is flawed, any actor could spoof transaction origins.

The RLP encoding and decoding of blocks, transactions, and headers is deterministic and collision-free. Multiple subgraphs rely on RLP for serialization (state_transition SG-003 checks len(rlp.encode(block)) > MAX_RLP_BLOCK_SIZE). Non-deterministic encoding could cause consensus splits.

Keccak256 hash function is collision-resistant. Block hash computation (get_last_256_block_hashes SG-002), parent hash verification (validate_header SG-005), and state/receipt/transaction root computations all depend on keccak256. A collision would allow block forgery.

The consensus layer provides correct and timely withdrawal information. Withdrawals directly increase account balances (process_withdrawals SG-014) without additional EL-side validation of the withdrawal validity. A compromised CL could inject fraudulent withdrawals.

System contracts at BEACON_ROOTS_ADDRESS, HISTORY_STORAGE_ADDRESS, WITHDRAWAL_REQUEST_ADDRESS, and CONSOLIDATION_REQUEST_ADDRESS contain correct code deployed at the fork transition. The process_unchecked_system_transaction (SG-010) does NOT verify contract code existence or execution success for beacon roots and history storage.

The EIP-1559 base fee mechanism (calculate_base_fee_per_gas SG-004) correctly converges to equilibrium gas pricing. The minimum increase of 1 when gas exceeds target prevents base fee stagnation. The elasticity multiplier of 2 bounds maximum block utilization.

Transaction validation checks in the dispatch graph (SG-004 of exceptions.py) are ordered correctly from general to specific. Reordering validation checks could allow transactions that should be rejected to pass, or cause less informative errors to be raised before more critical ones.

The VERSIONED_HASH_VERSION_KZG constant (0x01) correctly identifies KZG-committed blob data. All blob versioned hashes must have this version prefix. Accepting a different version prefix could allow uncommitted or incorrectly committed blob data.

Gas refund is correctly capped at gas_used // 5 (EIP-3529) and the calldata floor gas cost (EIP-7623) is enforced. Incorrect refund calculation could allow transactions to receive more gas back than intended, creating an economic exploit.

The MAX_RLP_BLOCK_SIZE (EIP-7934) limit is checked before any block processing to prevent resource exhaustion. If this check can be bypassed or the limit is set incorrectly, oversized blocks could cause memory exhaustion or excessive processing time on receiving nodes.

Post-merge Ethereum invariants are correctly enforced: difficulty == 0, nonce == 0x0000000000000000, ommers == empty tuple, ommers_hash == EMPTY_OMMER_HASH. Failure to enforce these allows pre-merge semantics to be invoked in a post-merge context.

The Engine API (JWT-authenticated) is not exposed to untrusted networks. Engine API is semi-trusted and used for CL-EL communication. If exposed publicly, an attacker could inject malicious block parameters or withdrawal lists.

Sender accounts with code (smart contract accounts) must have a valid delegation designation to send transactions (check_transaction SG-006, INV-011). This prevents contracts from arbitrarily initiating transactions unless specifically delegated via EIP-7702.

The chain retains at most 255 blocks after pruning (state_transition SG-003, INV-004). This bounds memory usage but assumes that 255 blocks is sufficient for all historical lookups (get_last_256_block_hashes SG-002).

stride_analysis.description: An attacker crafts a transaction with a forged sender identity by manipulating the ECDSA signature (v, r, s). If recover_sender has an edge-case bug (e.g., handling of s-value malleability, recovery of invalid signatures), the attacker could impersonate another account and spend its funds or increment its nonce.
stride_analysis.mitigation: Signature recovery via ecrecover must reject malleable signatures (enforce low-s). check_transaction (SG-006) calls recover_sender which derives the canonical sender address. EIP-2 already enforces s-value restrictions.

stride_analysis.description: An attacker submits a transaction with carefully crafted gas parameters to exploit edge cases in fee calculation. For example, setting max_fee_per_gas to values near the uint256 boundary could cause integer overflow in effective_gas_fee = tx.gas * effective_gas_price (process_transaction SG-013). Similarly, blob_gas_fee calculations could overflow.
stride_analysis.mitigation: Python execution specs use arbitrary-precision integers (Uint type), but client implementations in Go/Rust/Java may use fixed-width integers. The Uint type wrapping and the check_transaction validation guards (SG-006) should prevent overflow, but implementation-specific overflows are the primary concern.

stride_analysis.description: An attacker submits transactions designed to maximize validation cost while being rejected. The transaction_validation_dispatch (SG-004 of exceptions.py) performs up to 13 sequential checks. If early checks are cheap but later checks (e.g., blob versioned hash iteration) are expensive, an attacker could craft transactions that pass early checks but fail on expensive later ones, wasting validator CPU.
stride_analysis.mitigation: Validation checks are ordered general-to-specific (INV-003 of SG-004 exceptions.py). Transaction type check is first, then fee checks, then blob-specific checks. However, the blob hash iteration loop could be expensive with MAX_BLOB_COUNT hashes.

stride_analysis.description: An attacker sends an oversized RLP-encoded block via P2P. The state_transition function (SG-003) checks len(rlp.encode(block)) > MAX_RLP_BLOCK_SIZE as the first validation step (EIP-7934). However, the node must first receive and RLP-decode the block to compute its size, which itself consumes memory and CPU. If the RLP decoding happens before the size check, a sufficiently large malformed block could exhaust memory before the check triggers.
stride_analysis.mitigation: EIP-7934 introduces MAX_RLP_BLOCK_SIZE (approximately 8MB). Implementations should enforce a receive-level size limit before attempting full RLP decode. The size check is the first operation in state_transition (INV-001 of SG-003).

stride_analysis.description: An attacker proposes a block with inconsistent header fields (e.g., gas_used != computed gas, state_root != computed root). The state_transition function (SG-003) verifies 8 separate root/hash comparisons. If any comparison can be bypassed (e.g., due to a hash collision or comparison bug), the attacker could commit an invalid state transition that is accepted by the network, leading to a consensus split.
stride_analysis.mitigation: All 8 computed roots/hashes must match header values exactly (INV-003 of SG-003). The validate_header function (SG-005) performs 10+ additional header field validations. Both functions raise InvalidBlock on any mismatch.

stride_analysis.description: An attacker creates a block with a parent_hash that matches a different block's hash (hash collision). validate_header (SG-005) checks header.parent_hash == keccak256(rlp.encode(parent_header)). While keccak256 collisions are computationally infeasible, an implementation bug in the hash comparison or parent lookup could allow chain reorganization attacks.
stride_analysis.mitigation: Keccak256 is collision-resistant (256-bit security). Parent hash verification is performed cryptographically (INV-008 of SG-005). The check is performed after block number sequentiality check (INV-003).

stride_analysis.description: A compromised or buggy CL sends incorrect parent_beacon_block_root to the EL via Engine API. The apply_body function (SG-011) passes this directly to process_unchecked_system_transaction targeting BEACON_ROOTS_ADDRESS (SG-010). Since the system transaction is unchecked, no validation is performed on whether the beacon root is correct. A wrong beacon root would corrupt the beacon roots ring buffer, which is used by smart contracts relying on EIP-4788.
stride_analysis.mitigation: Engine API is JWT-authenticated. The CL is assumed to be a co-located trusted process. However, the unchecked nature of this system transaction (INV-001 of SG-010) means no EL-side verification occurs. A compromised CL can silently corrupt beacon root data.

stride_analysis.description: System transactions execute with SYSTEM_ADDRESS as origin and fixed gas of 30,000,000 (SYSTEM_TRANSACTION_GAS). If a system contract (WITHDRAWAL_REQUEST_ADDRESS, CONSOLIDATION_REQUEST_ADDRESS) has a vulnerability, the high gas allowance and special origin could amplify the impact. The process_checked_system_transaction (SG-009) validates contract code existence and execution success, but does not validate the semantic correctness of the return data.
stride_analysis.mitigation: System contracts are specified in the protocol and deployed at fork activation. should_transfer_value is False and value is 0 (INV-003, INV-004 of SG-008). The checked variant ensures code exists and execution succeeds. However, the correctness of return data (withdrawal/consolidation requests) is assumed.

stride_analysis.description: A malicious proposer sets post-merge header fields to non-zero values (difficulty != 0, nonce != 0, ommers != empty) to trigger pre-merge code paths or cause validation divergence between clients. validate_header (SG-005) checks these invariants, but if a client implementation has a code path that processes non-zero difficulty before the validation check, it could lead to unexpected behavior.
stride_analysis.mitigation: validate_header (SG-005) enforces difficulty == 0 (INV-005), nonce == 0 (INV-006), ommers_hash == EMPTY_OMMER_HASH (INV-007). state_transition (SG-003) also checks ommers == () (INV-002). Double enforcement provides defense-in-depth.

stride_analysis.description: An attacker crafts a transaction to exploit the gas refund mechanism. The gas refund is capped at gas_used // 5 (EIP-3529), and then the calldata floor gas cost (EIP-7623) is enforced via max(gas_used - refund, calldata_floor_gas_cost). If the calldata floor calculation has an off-by-one error or the max() comparison is incorrect, the attacker could receive more refund than intended or pay less than the floor cost.
stride_analysis.mitigation: process_transaction (SG-013) enforces INV-001 (refund cap at gas_used//5) and INV-002 (EIP-7623 calldata floor). The sequential application of refund then floor ensures the minimum charge is met. Both invariants must hold simultaneously.

stride_analysis.description: Transaction error types in exceptions.py (SG-001 through SG-003) store the original transaction data in their exception objects (e.g., self.transaction = transaction in TransactionTypeContractCreationError). If exception messages or objects are surfaced to external callers (e.g., via JSON-RPC error responses), transaction details could be disclosed to unauthorized parties.
stride_analysis.mitigation: Exception objects are used internally for validation. The format strings include dynamic type names (type(transaction).__name__) and fee values. JSON-RPC surfaces error messages, but JSON-RPC is out-of-scope for this bounty. Internal logging should sanitize transaction data.

stride_analysis.description: An attacker submits a blob transaction with versioned hashes that have the correct version prefix (0x01) but correspond to invalid or non-existent KZG commitments. The EL-side validation (blob_transaction_validation SG-005, check_transaction SG-006) only checks the version byte prefix, not the cryptographic validity of the KZG commitment. Full KZG verification happens at the CL/data availability layer.
stride_analysis.mitigation: The EL validates the version prefix (VERSIONED_HASH_VERSION_KZG) per INV-003 of SG-005. Full KZG proof verification is performed by the CL. However, if the CL does not properly verify, invalid blob data could be committed. This is a cross-layer trust boundary.

stride_analysis.description: An attacker submits blob transactions with the maximum number of blobs (MAX_BLOB_COUNT) at maximum blob gas fee to fill the blob gas limit. While this is by-design behavior, if MAX_BLOB_COUNT or the blob gas limit calculations have edge cases (e.g., the total_blob_gas exceeds the uint64 range for certain blob counts), it could cause processing errors.
stride_analysis.mitigation: blob_transaction_validation (SG-005) checks count <= MAX_BLOB_COUNT (INV-005) and total_blob_gas within limit. The fee sufficiency check (max_fee_per_blob_gas >= blob_base_fee) provides economic resistance. EIP-7918 (Blob base fee bounded by execution cost) provides additional protection.

stride_analysis.description: A SetCode transaction (EIP-7702) includes an authorization list that delegates code execution to a malicious contract. If the authorization list validation in check_transaction (SG-006) fails to properly verify the chain_id, nonce, or signature of each authorization entry, an attacker could gain code execution authority over accounts they do not control.
stride_analysis.mitigation: check_transaction (SG-006) validates that authorization lists are non-empty (INV-008). Sender code check (INV-011) ensures that accounts with code must have valid delegation. However, the detailed signature verification of individual authorization entries is handled by downstream processing not fully captured in this subgraph.

stride_analysis.description: An attacker sends blocks that are just under the MAX_RLP_BLOCK_SIZE limit (EIP-7934) but contain maximally complex transactions (e.g., deep nesting, large calldata). While the block passes the size check, processing it consumes disproportionate CPU/memory. The state_transition function (SG-003) must compute all 8 roots and verify the entire block even if it turns out to be invalid on later checks.
stride_analysis.mitigation: MAX_RLP_BLOCK_SIZE provides a hard upper bound on block size. Transaction gas limits (EIP-7825) cap per-transaction gas. The validation order (size check first, then header validation, then full execution) minimizes wasted work on obviously invalid blocks. However, blocks that pass header validation but fail on state root take maximum processing time.

stride_analysis.description: The process_general_purpose_requests function (SG-012) assembles requests in a fixed order: deposits, then withdrawals, then consolidations. Deposit requests are parsed from block output logs (not from system transactions), while withdrawal and consolidation requests come from checked system transactions. If the log parsing for deposits is incorrect (e.g., parsing logs from the wrong address or with incorrect ABI decoding), fraudulent deposit requests could be injected.
stride_analysis.mitigation: Deposit requests are parsed from block output logs via parse_deposit_requests (INV-002 of SG-012). The request ordering invariant (INV-001) ensures deterministic assembly. Withdrawal and consolidation system transactions are checked (must succeed, INV-003). The requests_hash in the block header (verified by state_transition SG-003) provides a final integrity check.

stride_analysis.description: A malicious proposer manipulates the gas limit to gradually increase or decrease it beyond intended bounds. The check_gas_limit function (SG-015) bounds changes to parent_gas_limit // 1024 (~0.1% per block) and enforces GAS_LIMIT_MINIMUM (5000). Over many blocks, a colluding set of proposers could drift the gas limit to extreme values. The calculate_base_fee_per_gas function (SG-004 fork.py) relies on gas limit for target calculation.
stride_analysis.mitigation: check_gas_limit (SG-015) enforces three constraints: upper bound, lower bound, and minimum (INV-001, INV-002, INV-003). The ~0.1% per block rate of change means significant drift requires sustained control of block production. The base fee adjustment (INV-001 of SG-004) provides economic feedback.

stride_analysis.description: Transaction execution results are recorded in receipts (make_receipt SG-007) and transaction tries. The succeeded field is set to (error is None), and logs are captured. However, if a transaction execution produces side effects that are not fully captured in the receipt (e.g., selfdestruct operations recorded in accounts_to_delete), there could be state changes that are difficult to audit after the fact.
stride_analysis.mitigation: make_receipt (SG-007) records success status, cumulative gas used, bloom filter, and logs (INV-001, INV-002). The receipt trie root in the block header provides cryptographic commitment to all receipts. Account deletions (process_transaction SG-013, INV-007) are deterministic.

stride_analysis.description: An attacker submits a transaction with a nonce that matches the sender's current nonce but exploits a race condition or reentrancy in nonce management. The process_transaction function (SG-013) increments the nonce BEFORE message execution (INV-006). If the nonce increment and balance deduction are not atomic with the execution, a reentrancy attack could allow the sender to submit multiple transactions with the same nonce.
stride_analysis.mitigation: Nonce is incremented before execution (INV-006 of SG-013). check_transaction (SG-006) enforces exact nonce match (INV-009). Balance deduction occurs before execution. These operations are sequential within a single-threaded block execution model.

stride_analysis.description: If the Gwei-to-Wei scaling factor (10^9) in withdrawal processing is implemented incorrectly (e.g., off by a factor due to a constant error), withdrawal amounts could be inflated or deflated. The process_withdrawals function (SG-014) scales amounts by 10^9 and only increases balances (INV-001, INV-002). An incorrect scaling factor could create or destroy ETH.
stride_analysis.mitigation: The scaling is specified as a protocol constant (10^9 Wei per Gwei, INV-001 of SG-014). Balance is always increased, never decreased (INV-002). The state root verification in state_transition (SG-003) ensures all clients agree on the final state.

stride_analysis.description: If a system contract (WITHDRAWAL_REQUEST_ADDRESS or CONSOLIDATION_REQUEST_ADDRESS) reverts during a checked system transaction, the entire block is invalid (process_checked_system_transaction SG-009, INV-002). An attacker who can cause the system contract to revert (e.g., by depleting its storage or gas) could prevent all blocks from being valid, halting the chain.
stride_analysis.mitigation: System transactions use fixed gas of 30,000,000 (SYSTEM_TRANSACTION_GAS, INV-002 of SG-008). System contracts are carefully designed to not revert under normal conditions. The code existence check (INV-001 of SG-009) prevents calling empty addresses. However, a bug in the system contract code could cause unexpected reverts.

stride_analysis.description: validate_header (SG-005) checks excess_blob_gas via calculate_excess_blob_gas(parent) before other validations. If the excess blob gas calculation has an edge case (e.g., underflow when target_blob_gas > parent_blob_gas_used), it could produce incorrect results, causing valid blocks to be rejected or invalid blocks to be accepted. This is especially relevant for EIP-7892 (Blob Parameter Only Hardforks) and EIP-7918 (Blob base fee bounded by execution cost).
stride_analysis.mitigation: validate_header (SG-005) checks excess_blob_gas (INV-009) and gas_used <= gas_limit (INV-010). The calculation depends on the parent block's blob gas usage. Underflow protection via Uint type is assumed. Cross-client consistency is verified via the state root check.

stride_analysis.description: The exception classes (SG-001 through SG-003) use Python f-string formatting with user-controlled values (transaction_type, type(transaction).__name__, fee values). If any client implementation uses a format string mechanism vulnerable to injection (e.g., log format string injection), attacker-controlled transaction fields could be used for injection attacks.
stride_analysis.mitigation: Python f-strings are not vulnerable to format string injection. However, client implementations in other languages (Go fmt.Sprintf, Java String.format) may be vulnerable if user-controlled values are used as format specifiers rather than arguments. The values are integers and type names, limiting the attack surface.

stride_analysis.description: The calculate_base_fee_per_gas function (SG-004 fork.py) guarantees a minimum increase of 1 when gas exceeds target (INV-002). When gas is below target, the decrease delta is gas_delta // DENOMINATOR, which can be 0 if the delta is small. This asymmetry means the base fee has an upward bias, which could be exploited by a proposer who alternates between full and empty blocks to manipulate the base fee in a specific direction.
stride_analysis.mitigation: The asymmetry is by design (EIP-1559). The minimum increase of 1 prevents base fee from getting stuck at 0. The DENOMINATOR provides a dampening factor. Economic incentives discourage base fee manipulation since proposers receive only priority fees.
